title: Q&A: What change of power in Colombia could mean for world's fossil-fuel transition - Carbon Brief
gist: Colombia's hard-right president-elect Abelardo de la Espriella, who has pledged to boost oil production and 'fracking to the max', ends the Petro era in which Colombia was the first major oil producer to halt new fossil-fuel expansion. The 'Santa Marta process' — a 57-country coalition championing fossil-fuel phase-out — now faces leadership uncertainty ahead of COP31 in Turkey in November 2026. The Netherlands and rotating co-hosts Tuvalu and Ireland aim to insulate the movement from political shocks through institutional resilience.

themes.theme: Colombia's Political Reversal on Fossil Fuels

insights.claim: Colombia under President Gustavo Petro became the first major oil-and-gas producer to commit to halting all new fossil-fuel expansion, a policy that will be reversed under incoming hard-right president Abelardo de la Espriella.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: A major emerging-market oil exporter is pivoting from fossil-fuel restriction to aggressive expansion, signaling production upside for Colombian oil and gas assets.
insights.quote: Under the leadership of leftist politician and economist Gustavo Petro, it became the first major oil-and-gas producer to commit to halting all new fossil-fuel expansion.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella has pledged to quickly boost new fossil-fuel and mining projects, including 'fracking to the max', representing a 180-degree policy reversal from the Petro government.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Colombian fracking and mining sector could see significant regulatory easing, creating investment opportunities in previously restricted upstream assets.
insights.quote: new hard-right populist president Abelardo de la Espriella has promised to quickly boost new fossil-fuel and mining projects, including by 'fracking to the max'.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: De la Espriella is also a favourite of US president Donald Trump, suggesting the new Colombian government will align more closely with US energy policy priorities favoring fossil fuel expansion.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: US-Colombia alignment on energy policy may unlock American investment in Colombian fossil fuel projects and reduce bilateral climate friction.
insights.quote: a hard-right populist and favourite of US president Donald Trump, who has pledged to boost oil production and pursue 'fracking to the max'.

insights.claim: De la Espriella has promised to build 10 'mega prisons' inside Colombia's Amazon rainforest, signaling a broader rollback of environmental protections beyond energy policy.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Amazon deforestation rates in Colombia may accelerate under the new government, heightening ESG and carbon credit risks for investors with Colombia exposure.
insights.quote: De la Espriella has also promised to build 10 'mega prisons' inside Colombia's Amazon rainforest.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Under Petro, Colombia became the first major oil exporter to sign the fossil-fuel non-proliferation treaty at COP28 in 2023, a commitment likely to be abandoned under the new administration.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The fossil-fuel non-proliferation treaty loses its first major oil-exporter signatory, weakening the legal framework's credibility and international momentum.
insights.quote: At the COP28 summit in 2023, Petro announced that Colombia would become the first major oil exporter to sign the fossil-fuel non-proliferation treaty.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

themes.theme: Santa Marta Process: Institutional Resilience vs. Political Risk

insights.claim: The 'Santa Marta process' assembled 57 countries representing one-third of the world's economy at a April 2026 fossil-fuel transition summit, giving it substantial coalition weight that could survive Colombia's political shift.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The breadth of the Santa Marta coalition (one-third of global GDP) creates a meaningful counterweight to major-economy opposition to fossil-fuel phase-out.
insights.quote: Some 57 countries – representing one-third of the world's economy – participated in the event.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: The Santa Marta process is designed with rotating presidencies and chairmanships — a new pair of co-host countries each year — specifically to insulate the movement from political shocks in any single country.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The structural design of the Santa Marta process makes it more durable than single-country-led climate commitments, reducing political-reversal risk to global fossil-fuel phase-out momentum.
insights.quote: We know that every couple of years there will be elections. That is why [we have] the idea of rotating presidencies and chairmanships…while we make sure we make use of existing secretariats and organisations that are not subject to political changes every couple of years.
insights.locator: Section: How could it affect international efforts to transition away from fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Outgoing Colombia environment minister Irene Vélez Torres argued that the legacy of the Petro government's climate work 'cannot be erased' and 'goes beyond governments', indicating she views institutional entrenchment as a buffer against political reversal.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The durability of energy-transition policy commitments depends on how deeply they are embedded in multilateral institutions, not just national governments.
insights.quote: Right now, we are facing the dark nights, this will really shift the politics in terms of energy position and environmental protection. But we are certain that our legacy will continue. It goes beyond governments.
insights.locator: Section: How could it affect international efforts to transition away from fossil fuels?

insights.claim: The second fossil-fuel transition summit will be co-hosted by Tuvalu and Ireland in 2027, confirming the Santa Marta process continues even without Colombia's involvement.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Investor attention to fossil-fuel phase-out momentum should shift from individual government positions to the health of multilateral forums like the Santa Marta process.
insights.quote: The meeting concluded with a range of outcomes, including a second fossil-fuel transition summit to be co-hosted by Tuvalu and Ireland in 2027.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: There are few major oil producers besides Colombia that have shown the same level of commitment to transitioning away from fossil fuels, making the leadership vacuum from Colombia's political shift difficult to fill.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The credibility of fossil-fuel phase-out coalitions depends on major oil producers joining them; Colombia's reversal highlights the scarcity of such willing producers and undermines coalition impact.
insights.quote: Although new countries could drive the process forward without Colombia, there are few major oil producers that have shown the same level of commitment to transitioning away from fossil fuels.
insights.locator: Section: How could it affect international efforts to transition away from fossil fuels?

insights.claim: The Brazilian COP30 presidency committed to releasing a voluntary (not formally negotiated) international fossil-fuel roadmap ahead of COP31 in Turkey in November 2026, informed by the Santa Marta summit takeaways.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: A voluntary rather than legally binding fossil-fuel roadmap limits enforcement mechanisms and reduces near-term mandatory capex obligations for oil majors.
insights.quote: The COP30 presidency is due to release a voluntary international 'fossil-fuel roadmap' ahead of COP31 in Turkey in November.
insights.locator: Section: How could efforts to transition away from fossil fuels feature at COP31?

insights.claim: Colombia and the Netherlands have had 'constructive' conversations with COP31 co-presidencies Australia and Turkey about incorporating Santa Marta fossil-fuel transition discussions, but both sides remain unsure how this will work in practice.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The inclusion of fossil-fuel phase-out language in COP31 formal texts is uncertain, lowering the probability of binding new commitments that could affect energy-sector capex or regulation.
insights.quote: they were keen to emphasise that all the conversations in Colombia were specifically not negotiations.
insights.locator: Section: How could efforts to transition away from fossil fuels feature at COP31?

insights.claim: Countries failed to agree to a formally negotiated fossil-fuel roadmap at COP30, with Brazil only promising a voluntary roadmap instead — setting a low bar for binding outcomes at COP31.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Back-to-back failures to codify fossil-fuel phase-out in formal COP texts suggests regulatory risk for oil and gas producers remains lower than climate-advocacy rhetoric implies.
insights.quote: countries failed to agree to a formally negotiated 'fossil-fuel roadmap' at COP30. However, the Brazilian COP30 presidency promised to bring forward a voluntary roadmap instead.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: It is unclear whether the 57-country Santa Marta group will appear as a formal collective at COP31 press conferences or events, signaling uncertainty about the coalition's diplomatic weight at the summit.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Without a formal Santa Marta bloc at COP31, fossil-fuel phase-out advocacy loses organized diplomatic pressure at a critical multilateral forum.
insights.quote: they were unsure of whether the group of 57 countries that gathered in Santa Marta would appear as a collective at press conferences or events at the COP31 summit.
insights.locator: Section: How could efforts to transition away from fossil fuels feature at COP31?

insights.claim: Under Petro, Colombia experienced a sustained decline in deforestation alongside a boost in renewable energy, making it an unusual case of environmental progress in a major oil-producing nation.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Colombia's climate progress under Petro shows that resource-rich developing nations can simultaneously reduce fossil-fuel dependence and deforestation when political will exists, but this is highly policy-dependent.
insights.quote: Under his leadership, Colombia became the first major oil producer and exporter to halt all new fossil-fuel expansion, boosted renewable energy and saw a sustained decline in deforestation.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Successive Colombian environment ministers under Petro, including Susana Muhamad and Irene Vélez Torres, were among the most vocal supporters of fossil-fuel transition at UN climate talks, giving Colombia outsized diplomatic influence relative to its economic size.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Colombia's loss from the pro-transition bloc reduces high-profile diplomatic pressure on larger emitters at climate negotiations.
insights.quote: Successive Colombian environment ministers became among the most vocal supporters of transitioning away from fossil fuels at UN climate talks.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Former Colombia environment minister Susana Muhamad stepped in to lead the 2024 UN biodiversity summit after original host Turkey withdrew following earthquakes, illustrating Colombia's elevated international climate profile under Petro.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Colombia's high-profile multilateral climate diplomacy under Petro will be difficult to replace, and the departure signals a material weakening of Global South leadership on climate ambition.
insights.quote: former minister Susana Muhamad, a political scientist and environmentalist who stepped in to lead the most recent UN biodiversity summit in 2024 after original Turkey was forced to withdraw following earthquakes.
insights.locator: Section: How could the election defeat change Colombia's stance on fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Ana Toni, CEO of the COP30 summit, stated that 'not one country will stop this movement' on fossil-fuel transition, arguing that collective global action is more resilient than any single leader.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: Diffuse multilateral ownership of fossil-fuel transition goals may be more durable than single-champion models, but also more difficult to coordinate into binding agreements.
insights.quote: Not one country will stop this movement. All countries need to chip in. There isn't one leader for this topic. Everybody needs to join forces.
insights.locator: Section: How could it affect international efforts to transition away from fossil fuels?

insights.claim: Colombia co-hosted the Santa Marta fossil-fuel transition summit with the Netherlands in April 2026, with the Netherlands positioned as the key institutional anchor for the process going forward after Colombia's political reversal.
insights.speaker: Daisy Dunne
insights.so_what: The Netherlands' continued co-leadership of the Santa Marta process makes Dutch climate policy a key determinant of international fossil-fuel transition momentum through at least 2027.
insights.quote: In April, the nation hosted a first-of-its-kind meeting of countries on transitioning away from fossil fuels, alongside the Netherlands, in the Caribbean city of Santa Marta.
